summary.text: [0-2] Stacked Nutrition Facts labels occupy the left, while the green NutritionFacts.org logo and tagline sit right against a white background. A scientific document slides up from the bottom, partially covering the labels. It displays "Review Article" and the title "Diagnosis and Treatment of Neurocysticercosis" by Christina M. Coyle and Herbert B. Tanowitz. [4-6] A bright yellow highlight appears over the phrase "the most common cause of acquired epilepsy worldwide" in the text. [6-12] The scene remains static, showing the highlighted article at the bottom with the nutrition labels and logo unchanged in the background.

[12-20] A gross pathology image of a brain cross-section containing multiple cysts slides in from the right, overlaying author names and a green logo. Yellow highlighted text stating "the most common cause of acquired epilepsy worldwide" remains visible at the bottom, while nutrition labels persist on the left. The scene remains static with the brain image fixed on the right.
[20-24] A new document titled "Neurocysticercosis: Fireflies in the Central Nervous System" by Petra N. Davidson replaces the previous scene. The page displays an abstract discussing headaches and seizures, affiliation details for Immanuel St. Joseph's – Mayo Health System, and journal citation "Am J Electroneurodiagnostic Technol 50:26-36, 2010" top left. Prior elements are removed.

[24-36] Static overlay on 'Neurocysticercosis: Fireflies in the Central Nervous System'. Text: 'Cysticerci create cavities in the brain and other body tissue where their tiny bodies grow sometimes into tapeworms two to seven meters in length and can live up to 25 years in the human body. On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) wormholes appear. Often times, if best practice is followed, the patient's entire body is scanned and more craters appear. Some authors have stated that cysticerci on a computed topography (CT) scan are similar to a starry sky or fireflies (Kapur et al. 2007).'

[36-48] Static scene. A white, torn-edge text box overlays the center containing: "Cysticerci create cavities in the brain and other body tissue where their tiny bodies grow sometimes into tapeworms two to seven meters in length and can live up to 25 years in the human body. On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) wormholes appear. Often times, if best practice is followed, the patient's entire body is scanned and more craters appear. Some authors have stated that cysticerci on a computed topography (CT) scan are similar to a starry sky or fireflies (Kapur et al. 2007)." Underlying document titled "Neurocysticercosis: Fireflies in the Central Nervous System" remains fixed.

[48-52] The document "Neurocysticercosis: Fireflies in the Central Nervous System" shows a text overlay where "wormholes" is highlighted yellow before the paper warps. [52-56] Scene cuts to a black background with an axial brain scan on the left with dark spots; a small tilted text strip appears lower right. [56-58] It expands into a large white text box over the scan, highlighting "similar to a starry sky" in yellow. [58-62] The text disappears, and the brain scan moves right, displaying numerous bright white spots, remaining static with the "BRAIN" label.

[64-70] Against a black background, an axial brain scan featuring bright spots remains static on the right. A white, torn-edge text strip on the left displays: "Each star is a fluid filled cyst with the beginning of a living, growing tapeworm inside." [70-72] A gross pathology image of a brain cross-section, showing yellowish tissue with cysts, appears centrally. It overlays the white text strip and partially obscures the MRI scan. [72-76] The yellowish pathology cross-section remains centered, obscuring the middle screen while text fragments remain visible on the edges. A small, dark, circular object appears on the right side of the pathology specimen.

[62-64] Axial brain scan featuring bright white spots remains static on the right against black. A thin white diagonal line segment appears on the left. [76-82] A large grey microscopic image with a central circular structure surrounded by sharp spikes overlays the yellowish brain cross-section. This textured object dominates the center, while the blue MRI scan remains visible. Small white text fragments "a li" and "f" remain visible. [82-86] A white document titled "Public Health Implications of Cysticercosis Acquired in the United States" rises from the bottom, obscuring the grey spiky image. Authors Frank Sorvillo, Patricia Wilkins, Shira Shafir, and Mark Eberhard are visible. A "Medscape CME ACTIVITY" box with fine print sits below, covering the prior collage.

[86-92] The white document titled "Public Health Implications of Cysticercosis Acquired in the United States" remains static. At the bottom, two text columns are visible; the left reads "Cysticercosis has emerged as a cause of severe neurologic disease," and the right begins "1954–2005. A confirmed or presumptive source of infection." [92-98] A yellow highlight appears over the bottom-left text, emphasizing "Cysticercosis has emerged as a cause of severe neurologic disease in the United States that primarily affects". The document remains otherwise unchanged.

[98-102] A white document titled "Public Health Implications of Cysticercosis Acquired in the United States" is displayed with a yellow highlight on the bottom text "Cysticercosis has emerged as a cause of severe neurologic disease in the United States that primarily affects." [102-110] A white rectangular text strip with torn edges appears centrally, defining Neurocysticercosis (NCC) caused by the pork tapeworm, Taenia solium, and listing symptoms like seizures. The underlying document and yellow highlight remain visible and static.

[110-124] The scene remains static, displaying a document titled "Public Health Implications of Cysticercosis Acquired in the United States." A central white text strip defines Neurocysticercosis (NCC) caused by the pork tapeworm *Taenia solium*, noting symptoms like seizures. At the bottom, yellow highlighted text states "Cysticercosis has emerged as a cause of severe neurologic disease in the United States." No visual changes or movements occur throughout the duration.

[24-36] Static overlay on 'Neurocysticercosis: Fireflies in the Central Nervous System'. Text: 'Cysticerci create cavities in the brain and other body tissue where their tiny bodies grow sometimes into tapeworms two to seven meters in length and can live up to 25 years in the human body. On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) wormholes appear. Often times, if best practice is followed, the patient's entire body is scanned and more craters appear. Some authors have stated that cysticerci on a computed topography (CT) scan are similar to a starry sky or fireflies (Kapur et al. 2007).'

Prior elements are removed.

[24-36] Static overlay on 'Neurocysticercosis: Fireflies in the Central Nervous System'. Text: 'Cysticerci create cavities in the brain and other body tissue where their tiny bodies grow sometimes into tapeworms two to seven meters in length and can live up to 25 years in the human body. On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) wormholes appear. Often times, if best practice is followed, the patient's entire body is scanned and more craters appear. Some authors have stated that cysticerci on a computed topography (CT) scan are similar to a starry sky or fireflies (Kapur et al. 2007).'
